prompt: The quicksort implementation is very slow on already-sorted arrays. It picks the first element as pivot, causing O(n^2) on sorted input. Also, it stack-overflows on large inputs.

turns.content:
<task>
The quicksort implementation is very slow on already-sorted arrays. It picks the first element as pivot, causing O(n^2) on sorted input. Also, it stack-overflows on large inputs.
</task>

arguments.reasoning:
Two issues:
1. O(n^2) on sorted input: pivot is always arr[0] (line 11). On sorted arrays, this always picks the minimum, creating one partition of size n-1 and one of size 0.
2. Stack overflow: O(n) recursion depth on sorted input with first-element pivot.

Fixes:
1. Use median-of-three pivot selection (first, middle, last)
2. Fall back to insertion sort for small subarrays (eliminates deep recursion on small partitions)

arguments.summary: Found both issues in src/lib.rs. Line 11: first-element pivot causes O(n^2) on sorted input. Deep recursion (O(n) depth) causes stack overflow. Fix: median-of-three pivot + insertion sort fallback for small arrays.

turns.content:
running 4 tests

test result: ok. 4 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored

arguments.summary: All 4 tests pass including test_sorted_input and test_large_array (no stack overflow).
arguments.final_answer:
Fixed quicksort in src/lib.rs:
1. Pivot: replaced first-element selection with median-of-three (first, middle, last). Avoids O(n^2) on sorted input.
2. Stack depth: insertion sort fallback for arrays <= 16 elements. Reduces recursion depth and improves cache locality.
All 4 tests pass including sorted input and large array tests.
